concepts.title: Prepration of Complex compounds
concepts.conceptBody:
Preparations of complex compounds

Complex compounds are generally prepared by the following methods:

(i) Substitution reaction in aq. solutions 

This method involves a reaction between a metal salt in aq solution and a coordinating agent, e.g. aq. solution of $\text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ and excess $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ forms a $\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}$ ammine.

$\left[ \text{Cu}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{6}} \right]\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+4\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}\left[ \text{Cu}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{4}} \right]\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+6{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

(Blue) (Deep Blue)

(ii) Substitution reactions in the absence of solvent 

Direct reaction between anhydrous metal salt and liquid$\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ produces metal ammines, e.g.

$\text{NiC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+6\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3(1)}}\xrightarrow{{}}\left[ \text{Ni}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{6}} \right]\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$

Yellow Violet

(iii) Redox reactions 

Co(II) salt forms a $\text{Co}$ (III) complex

$4\left[ \text{Co}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{6}} \right]\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+4\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Cl}+20\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}4\left[ \text{Co}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{6}} \right]\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{3}}+26{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

concepts.title: Stability of Complex Compound
concepts.conceptBody:
Stability of complex compound in solution

Complex formation can be seen as a Lewis acid - base reaction. The${{\text{M}}^{\text{n}+}}$ is Lewis acid and the ligands are Lewis bases. If the interaction between ${{\text{M}}^{\text{n}+}}$ and the ligands is strong, the complex is thermodynamically stable. The reaction between ${{\text{M}}^{\text{n}+}}$ and the ligands can be written as

${{\text{M}}^{\text{n}+}}+\text{x}{{\text{L}}^{\text{m}-}}\rightleftharpoons {{\left[ \text{M}{{\text{L}}_{\text{X}}} \right)}^{\text{p}+}}\text{, where }(\text{n}+)+(\text{m}-)=(\text{p}+)$

The equilibrium constant $\text{k}$ is stability constant and it is defined as

$\text{k}=\frac{{{\left[ \text{M}{{\text{L}}_{\text{X}}} \right]}^{\text{p}+}}}{\left[ {{\text{M}}^{\text{n}+}} \right]{{\left[ {{\text{L}}^{\text{m}-}} \right]}^{\text{X}}}}$

The numerical value of $\text{k}$ gives an indication of thermodynamic stability. Some stability constants are given below.

$\text{A}{{\text{g}}^{+}}+2\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\rightleftharpoons {{\left[ \text{Ag}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}} \right]}^{+}}\quad \,\,\,\,1.6\quad {{10}^{7}}$

$\text{C}{{\text{o}}^{3+}}+6\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\rightleftharpoons {{\left[ \text{Co}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{6}} \right]}^{3+}}\quad 5.3\quad {{10}^{33}}$

$\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}+4\text{C}{{\text{N}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons {{\left[ \text{Cu}{{\left( \text{C}{{\text{N}}_{4}} \right)}^{2-}} \right]}^{+}}\quad \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 2.0 & {{10}^{27}} \\ \end{array}$

$\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}+4\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\rightleftharpoons {{\left[ \text{Cu}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{4}} \right]}^{2+}}\quad 4.5\quad {{10}^{11}}$

The value of $\text{k}$ for $\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}$ complexes with $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ and $\text{C}{{\text{N}}^{-}}$show that cyano-complex is more stable than $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ complex.
